title: US Inflation Drops Sharply as Senate Blocks War Spending, New York Bans Data Centres, Indonesia Jails Gojek Founder
subhead: June CPI fell 0.4% monthly as energy prices plunged, but analysts warn the reprieve is already fading as Gulf oil climbs back above $80. Senate Democrats blocked a $1.15 trillion defence bill over Iran war powers and Israel intelligence-sharing provisions, while New York became the first US state to ban large data centre construction.

sections.heading: US Consumer Prices Tumbled in June, but the Reprieve May Be Over
sections.body:
US consumer prices fell 0.4 percent on a monthly basis in June, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported on Tuesday, the steepest monthly decline since the early months of the pandemic. Energy prices led the drop, tumbling 5.7 percent, with oil down 9.7 percent and petrol down 9.5 percent. The average US petrol price fell to $3.85 per gallon from $4.07 a month earlier, according to AAA data.

But the annual picture tells a different story: CPI rose 3.5 percent year-on-year after a 4.2 percent increase in May, which was the largest annual surge in over three years. Petrol prices were up 27 percent compared with a year ago, and shelter costs rose 3 percent.

Analysts say the monthly improvement is already being erased. Patrick De Haan, head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, noted that WTI crude is back above $80 and petrol is climbing again, with $4-per-gallon gas likely days away. Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who took the helm in May, told lawmakers the central bank has 'no tolerance for persistently elevated inflation.' CME FedWatch shows an 87.7 percent probability that rates stay at 3.5-3.75 percent at the next meeting, with the remaining probability on a 25-basis-point hike.
sections.whyItMatters: The CPI print gives the Fed a brief window of good news, but the Iran-Hormuz escalation has already reversed the energy price decline that drove it. The question is whether this month's numbers will look anything like June's when July data comes in.

sections.heading: Senate Democrats Block $1.15 Trillion Defence Bill Over Iran War and Israel Provisions
sections.body:
US Senate Democrats blocked debate on the National Defense Authorization Act in a 50-46 vote on Tuesday, almost entirely along party lines. The motion needed 60 votes to advance.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called the bill 'a permission slip' for the Trump administration to continue military operations against Iran without congressional oversight. A coalition of 14 civil liberty and antiwar organisations, including the ACLU and J Street, urged lawmakers to oppose the NDAA unless guaranteed a vote on barring funding for the Iran war.

Beyond the war, Democrats objected to provisions that would deepen US-Israel military and intelligence integration, including a requirement for the Pentagon to appoint an official to coordinate defence technology between the two countries. The bill also called for 'data fusion' between US and Israeli intelligence, which Human Rights Watch warned could involve mass surveillance programmes. A companion intelligence authorisation measure would expand intelligence-sharing with Israel.

Senators Chris Van Hollen, Bernie Sanders, Elizabeth Warren, Ed Markey, Jeff Merkley and Peter Welch urged colleagues to oppose the bill, citing both Iran war funding and Israel integration provisions. Israel's favourability among Democrats has dropped from 59 percent in 2018 to 22 percent in May, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll.
sections.whyItMatters: A blocked NDAA is rare and signals how the Iran war has fractured congressional war powers norms. If the impasse persists, it could delay defence spending and raise questions about legislative oversight of an ongoing military campaign.

sections.heading: New York Becomes First US State to Ban Large Data Centre Construction
sections.body:
Governor Kathy Hochul announced a one-year moratorium on new large data centre construction in New York State, the first such statewide ban in the US. The moratorium covers facilities using 50 megawatts or more of power and shifts operational costs onto developers, requiring them to pay more for electricity, contribute to grid upgrades, provide their own power, and invest in clean energy.

Hochul also said the state is moving to repeal sales tax exemptions for data centres. New York has 148 operational data centres, the sixth-largest concentration of any state.

The issue has become a political flashpoint ahead of midterms. A June Reuters/Ipsos poll found only 14 percent of Americans would be comfortable with a data centre near them, while a Gallup poll found 71 percent oppose local data centre construction, including 48 percent who strongly oppose it. Moratoriums have been proposed in at least a dozen states. In Utah, a state senate president who backed a data centre project was voted out in the June primary. A Reuters analysis found Elon Musk's xAI Colossus 2 project in Tennessee installed 59 natural gas turbines without federal clean air permits, disproportionately affecting nearby predominantly Black communities.

Research from UC Riverside projects data centres will contribute to 600,000 asthma-related symptom cases by the end of the decade.
sections.whyItMatters: New York's ban is a landmark in the collision between AI infrastructure demand and local resource constraints. If other states follow, the economics of data centre buildout — and by extension AI compute — face a regulatory wall that could reshape where and how rapidly the industry scales.

sections.heading: Indonesia's Gojek Founder Sentencing Shakes Investor Confidence as Rupiah Hits All-Time Low
sections.body:
Nadiem Makarim, cofounder of ride-hailing super-app Gojek and former Indonesian education minister, was sentenced to 10 years in prison for abusing his authority in a Chromebook procurement deal that allegedly favoured Google, an early Gojek investor. Prosecutors claimed Makarim inflicted $120 million in state losses by tailoring tender specifications for over 1 million laptops that would not work in remote areas with poor internet.

Critics see the prosecution as political retribution by President Prabowo Subianto's administration. A judge issued a dissenting opinion finding 'no evidence of malicious intent.' Makarim was acquitted of the charge of directly seeking to enrich himself and received a sentence below the 18 years prosecutors sought.

The case has rattled investors already wary of Prabowo's economic management. The Indonesian rupiah hit an all-time low against the US dollar in June, which analysts partly attributed to scepticism about his populist policies, including a free lunch programme expected to cost $15 billion this year. CSIS Jakarta researcher Nicky Fahrizal told Al Jazeera the case has served as 'a warning signal to investors,' while ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute's Siwage Dharma Negara said Indonesia's reputation as an investment destination was already declining due to 'government policies that are increasingly less pro-market.'
sections.whyItMatters: The jailing of one of Southeast Asia's most prominent tech entrepreneurs signals a worsening investment climate in Indonesia, the region's largest economy. The rupiah at all-time lows and the $15 billion free lunch programme raise questions about fiscal sustainability at a time when capital is already jittery about emerging markets.

sections.heading: Iran Lawmakers Push Hormuz Sovereignty Bill as US Reimposes Blockade
sections.body:
Iranian lawmakers introduced the 'Strategic Action for the Security and Sustainable Progress of the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf' during an unannounced parliamentary session on Monday night. Ebrahim Azizi, head of parliament's national security commission, said the bill is 'the first step' and 'subsequent measures are forthcoming.'

An earlier draft published in May proposed barring US and Israeli-flagged vessels from passage, requiring all ships to obtain permits, disclose cargo and pay fees, and mandating the name 'Persian Gulf' — with cargo confiscation of up to 20 percent for violations. It is unclear what changes have been made to the current text.

The legislative push comes as the US reimposed its naval blockade on Iranian ports and rescinded waivers for Iranian oil exports. The US military's Central Command announced a new wave of strikes on Iran, targeting areas near Bandar Abbas, Sirik, Qeshm Island and Kish Island. Iran's IRGC hit two supertankers in Omani waters, killing one crew member, and carried out retaliatory strikes on Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan. Trump replaced his proposed 20 percent Hormuz transit fee with pledges of 'massive' investment deals from Gulf states instead, telling reporters he does not 'like the concept of a fee.'

Iran's rial slid to 1.85 million per dollar in Tehran's open market, near May's all-time low of 1.9 million. The Tehran Stock Exchange lost another 42,000 points on Tuesday, staying below 5 million.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is moving to formalise its claim over Hormuz just as the US reimposes its blockade — both sides are hardening positions rather than seeking an off-ramp. The legislative push, if passed, would give Tehran a domestic legal framework for imposing passage fees, turning a wartime tactic into institutional policy.

sections.heading: Cuba Hit With New US Sanctions and Third Power Blackout in 10 Days
sections.body:
The US Treasury Department sanctioned Cuba's Ministry of Tourism and two state-owned companies — GEMAR (Grupo Empresarial de Transporte Maritimo Portuario) and GECOMEX (Grupo Empresarial del Comercio Exterior) — giving firms and financial institutions until August 12 to wind down contracts without facing penalties.

The sanctions follow a May executive order allowing the US to freeze assets of anyone supporting Cuba's government or economy and increased pressure on banks working with Cuban entities. UN Ambassador Mike Waltz called Cuba's regime 'a national security threat' and accused Russia and China of collecting intelligence near US military bases on the island.

Cuba is simultaneously reeling from its third nationwide blackout in 10 days, affecting over 10 million people. The power crisis stems from a US oil blockade imposed after the abduction of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro in January — Venezuela was Cuba's primary fuel supplier. Mexico also halted oil shipments under US pressure.

Cuban Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez Parrilla called the sanctions 'an act of collective punishment' and noted that US embargoes between March 2025 and February 2026 caused $8 billion in damage, on top of the 'extreme impact' of the fuel blockade.
sections.whyItMatters: The combined pressure of sanctions and energy blockade is pushing Cuba toward systemic failure. With the power grid collapsing repeatedly and financial institutions forced to cut ties, the sanctions architecture is achieving maximum economic disruption — but at the cost of humanitarian crisis for 10 million people.

sections.heading: Iraq Pivots to Economic Ties With US, Seeks Higher OPEC Quota
sections.body:
Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi met Trump at the White House on Tuesday, with both leaders pledging to deepen economic ties and boost Iraq's oil output. Al-Zaidi, a businessman with no prior political history whom Trump publicly backed over Iran-aligned rival Nouri al-Maliki, said US-Iraqi relations were shifting from militaristic to economic.

Trump vowed 'a lot of deals' and said the US would be 'taking out a lot of oil.' Both leaders confirmed that remaining US forces in Iraq — believed to be fewer than 2,000 troops — would withdraw completely by September 30, the same date al-Zaidi pledged armed factions would disarm.

Al-Zaidi also pushed for a higher OPEC production quota, citing $400 billion in war damage from the ISIL conflict that remains unrepaired. Iraq exports roughly 3.4 million barrels per day, with about 90 percent passing through the Strait of Hormuz — making it one of the economies most exposed to the waterway's disruption.

The Iran-backed Islamic Resistance in Iraq said it would reject any outcomes of the visit.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq is attempting a fundamental strategic realignment — from security client to energy partner — at precisely the moment when Hormuz disruption threatens 90 percent of its export revenue. Al-Zaidi's OPEC quota push and the September troop withdrawal deadline create a compressed timeline for Iraq to prove it can stabilise without US military backing.

bottomLine: June's CPI drop gave the Fed a fleeting reprieve, but oil is already climbing back as Iran and the US harden positions — Tehran is legislating Hormuz sovereignty while Washington reimposes a blockade. Domestically, the Senate's rare block of the defence bill signals deepening resistance to the Iran war and Israel intelligence integration, while New York's data centre ban opens a new front in the AI-versus-infrastructure clash. Indonesia's jailing of the Gojek founder and Cuba's cascading sanctions-and-blackout crisis show the pressure on emerging markets from political risk and US financial warfare respectively.
sources: Al Jazeera, Bureau of Labor Statistics (US CPI), CME FedWatch, GasBuddy, AAA, Reuters/Ipsos, Gallup, UC Riverside, CSIS Jakarta, ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute, US Treasury Department, UN World Food Programme
